message.content:
Implement EXACTLY task **T115** (goals:G6) end-to-end in your isolated worktree, base commit `636b149` (current main). If your worktree HEAD is not that commit, `git reset --hard 636b149` (sanctioned). Report your branch name + resultCommit.

**Headline:** Sync the config reference, wanbond.example.toml, design.md, and README for all new surfaces

**Description:** Per the AGENTS.md docs-in-sync rule, ONE final sweep after all the config-surface and behavior tasks landed. Ensure these NEW surfaces (all already shipped) are documented consistently across ALL docs:
- **`bind`** (per-path in [[paths]] AND top-level global default; default "auto"; values source/device/auto) — shipped T105/T106.
- **`mode = "default-route"`** (edge-only full-tunnel opt-in; daemon installs the /1+/1 split routes) + the 0.0.0.0/0→/1+/1 UAPI-render split semantics — shipped T107/T108.
- **`tun_persist`** (default false; TUNSETPERSIST; survives restart) — shipped T109.
- **`wanbond_session_established`** metric + the 'session established' log line — shipped T101.

**Acceptance:** Every new config key and metric introduced by this goal appears in install.md §3z (exhaustive key reference), wanbond.example.toml (commented-out with default, matching existing style), and docs/design.md; docs/runbook.md references the new C1 (NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in), C3 (full-tunnel client-LAN recipe), and C4 (addressing/persistence oneshot) provisioning steps; README feature list updated if it enumerates config keys or metrics; grep for each new key/metric name across docs/ (incl. runbook.md) and README returns the expected hits; NO stale 'never assigns routes' claim survives unqualified (design.md must note the default-route wiring as the ONE deliberate exception).

CRITICAL — this is a CONSISTENCY SWEEP, and much of this content ALREADY EXISTS from prior tasks (T79 added [dns]; T98 added multi-peer; T112 added §3b bind + the oif recipe; T113 added §9 full-tunnel + §5 C6; T114 added §6a; T108/T109/T110/T111 added mode/persist/NM/oneshot). So FIRST inventory what's already documented (grep each key/metric across docs/ + README + wanbond.example.toml), then fill ONLY the genuine GAPS. Do NOT duplicate existing sections. GROUND every statement in shipped source:
- `bind`/BindMode: internal/config/config.go (field shape, values, top-level default) + internal/bind/pathsock.go (selectDeviceBinds behavior).
- `mode`/split: internal/config/config.go (Peer.Mode, edge-only) + internal/device/device.go (splitDefaultRoute/uapiConfig, unconditional split) + internal/device/route_linux.go (route install).
- `tun_persist`: internal/device/persist_linux.go.
- session metric/log: internal/metrics/metrics.go (wanbond_session_established) + internal/device/session.go ('session established').
Be careful with exact metric/log strings (verbatim) and never claim a literal 0.0.0.0/0 is unsafe (the daemon splits it). If §3z / wanbond.example.toml already cover a key, verify it's accurate rather than re-adding.

If wanbond.example.toml already has a config test (TestExampleConfigLoads in internal/config), ensure any example you add/change still loads (extend the test if you add a materially new stanza).

Gate before commit: `nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...'` (green incl. TestExampleConfigLoads). Commit on your worktree branch. Return the standard structured result JSON {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary}. Do NOT mutate the ledger, do NOT merge. End with a `### Session summary` block.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

50:	// Bind is the OPTIONAL top-level default bind mode (I5, Q42) applied to every
51:	// path that omits its own `bind`. Left empty it defaults to BindModeAuto
53:	// with no `bind` anywhere keeps exactly today's per-path bind behaviour.
54:	Bind BindMode `toml:"bind"`
69:// BindMode selects, per path, how that path's UDP socket is bound to the
72://   - BindModeAuto reproduces today's selectDeviceBinds heuristic: device-bind
74://     source-IP-bind otherwise.
75://   - BindModeSource forces the pre-T16 source-IP pin unconditionally.
76://   - BindModeDevice forces a device bind unconditionally.

toolUseResult.stdout: 593:### 3z. Full configuration reference (all keys)

content.content:
593	### 3z. Full configuration reference (all keys)
594	
595	This is the exhaustive key list — every configuration key wanbond reads, in one
596	place. The example below is **edge-oriented**; concentrator-only keys are shown
597	and marked `CONCENTRATOR-ONLY`. Required keys are shown live; optional/defaulted
598	keys are shown **commented-out with their default value**. Uncommenting a key
599	and leaving it at the shown value is a no-op. The per-section notes after the
600	example capture the cross-field constraints a single example cannot express
601	inline. All of these are enforced at config load (`config.Load`), which fails
602	fast on the first violation — except the loopback and `allowed_ips` checks,
603	noted below.
604	
605	> The same content ships as a copy-pasteable file at the repo root,
606	> [`wanbond.example.toml`](../wanbond.example.toml): copy it, fill the
607	> `<placeholders>`, and delete what you do not need.
608	
609	```toml
610	# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
611	role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
612	                                   #   inferred from other keys.
613	psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. Same value
614	                                   #   on both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
615	                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
616	# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. false => wanbond0 is
617	                                   #   destroyed on daemon stop (its addresses/
618	                                   #   routes/rules drop on every restart). true =>
619	                                   #   TUNSETPERSIST on start: wanbond0 survives a
620	                                   #   restart with the SAME ifindex, so operator-
621	                                   #   owned addressing persists. See "Interface
622	                                   #   addressing" below for the NM (D39) caveat.
623	
624	# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
625	[[paths]]
626	name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
627	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's
628	                                   #   UDP socket binds to. Must be unique across
629	                                   #   paths (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE
630	                                   #   at the second bind). No default.
631	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
632	                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
633	                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the
634	                                   #   peer's endpoint is reused). No default.
635	                                   #   Inert on the concentrator (it learns edge
636	                                   #   endpoints from traffic).
637	# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
638	                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
639	                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
640	                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
641	                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
642	# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT
643	                                   #   (Go duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when
644	                                   #   link_bandwidth is set under weighted
645	                                   #   pacing; ignored otherwise. No default.
646	
647	# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
648	[[paths]]
649	name        = "5g"
650	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
651	
652	# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
653	[wireguard]
654	private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
655	# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there;
656	                                   #   omit / leave 0 on the edge. uint16
657	                                   #   (< 1024 needs CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).
658	
659	# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
660	[[wireguard.peers]]
661	public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key.
662	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`).
663	                                   #   ip:port literal (default) OR hostname
664	                                   #   with opt-in `dns = true` (§Optional
665	                                   #   [dns] resolver block). CONCENTRATOR:
666	                                   #   must be ABSENT (rejected) — it roams
667	                                   #   the edge dynamically. Mutually
668	                                   #   exclusive with `endpoints`.
669	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
670	                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is
671	                                   #   the active concentrator, the rest ordered
672	                                   #   standbys. Each entry may be an ip:port
673	                                   #   literal or, with `dns = true`, a
674	                                   #   hostname; duplicates rejected within
675	                                   #   each form. Mutually exclusive with
676	                                   #   `endpoint` (which is its one-element
677	                                   #   form). Rejected on the concentrator.
678	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
679	                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built). A
680	                                   #   literal 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 is always split
681	                                   #   into the equivalent /1+/1 pair at UAPI
682	                                   #   render.
683	# mode = "default-route"           # OPTIONAL, edge-only. Marks this peer as
684	                                   #   the edge's full-tunnel concentrator: the
685	                                   #   daemon installs this peer's allowed_ips
686	                                   #   split (wg-quick /1+/1 for 0.0.0.0/0) as
687	                                   #   routes via wanbond0 once the interface is
688	                                   #   up, and removes them on stop. Routes ONLY —
689	                                   #   no policy routing/SNAT/forwarding, which
690	                                   #   stay operator-owned (§4). Rejected on the
691	                                   #   concentrator.
692	
693	# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
694	# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire
695	# jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2 set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One engine per process.
696	# [amnezia]
697	# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
698	# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
699	# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
700	# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
701	# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
702	                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
703	                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must
704	                                   #   differ. (no defaults: required when block
705	                                   #   present.)
706	# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when
707	# h2 = 2                           #   the block is configured but NO header is
708	# h3 = 3                           #   given. When any is set, h1..h4 must be a
709	# h4 = 4                           #   distinct set (values <= 4 mean "standard
710	                                   #   message type").
711	
712	# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
713	# [scheduler]
714	# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted".
715	                                   #   Every knob below applies ONLY to
716	                                   #   "weighted"; under active-backup they are
717	                                   #   inert and left unset.
718	# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
719	                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate
720	                                   #   denominator and pacing refill rate. > 0.
721	# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
722	                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
723	# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below
724	                                   #   disengage_fraction * capacity. Must be in
725	                                   #   [0, engage_fraction) — the hysteresis band.
726	# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
727	                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
728	# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator
729	                                   #   time constant. > 0.
730	# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
731	                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
732	# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames).
733	                                   #   > 0 when pacing_enabled.
734	# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight
735	                                   #   formula. > 0.
736	# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in
737	                                   #   the weight formula. > 0.
738	
739	# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
740	# [fec]
741	# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
742	                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
743	# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1
744	                                   #   when enabled. No default (required).
745	# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
746	                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled;
747	                                   #   K + M <= 256. No default (required).
748	# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline.
749	                                   #   > 0 and <= 125ms.
750	# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
751	                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
752	                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
753	# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
754	                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1).
755	                                   #   No default. Mutually exclusive with
756	                                   #   safety_factor.
757	# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier
758	                                   #   >= 1. DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and
759	                                   #   NEITHER field is set. Must stay 0 in fixed
760	                                   #   mode. Mutually exclusive with
761	                                   #   target_residual.
762	
763	# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
764	[metrics]
765	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8,
766	                                   #   ::1, or a hostname resolving only to
767	                                   #   loopback); any other address is REFUSED
768	                                   #   when the endpoint binds (not at load).
769	
770	# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
771	[log]
772	level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
773	                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning" is
774	                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.
775	```
776	
777	**Cross-field constraints and role applicability (not expressible inline):**
778	
779	- **Roles.** `role` is required and never inferred. Edge-only:
780	  per-peer `endpoint`/`endpoints` (required on the edge, rejected on the
781	  concentrator) and the pacing declarations (`dest_addr` is edge-only in effect,
782	  but is not *rejected* on the concentrator — merely inert). Concentrator-only:
783	  `wireguard.listen_port` (required there, unused on the edge).
784	- **`endpoint` vs `endpoints`.** Mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the
785	  one-element form of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the
786	  concentrator must set neither. Each entry is an `ip:port` literal (default)
787	  or, with the peer's `dns = true` opt-in, a hostname resolved at runtime
788	  (never at config load); duplicates are rejected within each form
789	  (literal-vs-literal, hostname-vs-hostname) — see §Optional `[dns]` resolver
790	  block.
791	- **Distinct `source_addr`.** Each `[[paths]]` needs a unique, valid
792	  `source_addr` (compared unmapped, so `192.0.2.10` and `::ffff:192.0.2.10`
793	  collide). `name` must also be unique.
794	- **`allowed_ips`.** At least one CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG
795	  configuration is assembled (not by `config.validate`). A literal `0.0.0.0/0`
796	  or `::/0` (full tunnel) is ALWAYS split into the equivalent `/1`+`/1` pair at
797	  UAPI render — the engine never receives the literal `/0`, which wedges the
798	  handshake.
799	- **`mode` is edge-only.** Peer `mode = "default-route"` marks a peer as the
800	  edge's full-tunnel concentrator (an opt-in alongside a `0.0.0.0/0`/`::/0`
801	  `allowed_ips` entry); rejected on the concentrator, mirroring the
802	  `endpoint`/`dns` edge-only rules. When set, the daemon installs this peer's
803	  `allowed_ips` split (the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair for `0.0.0.0/0`) as scope-link
804	  routes via `wanbond0` once the interface is up, and withdraws them on stop —
805	  the daemon's ONLY route programming. It is deliberately minimal: routes ONLY,
806	  **no** client-LAN policy routing, SNAT, or concentrator `ip_forward`/
807	  `MASQUERADE`/`FORWARD` — those stay operator-owned recipes (see §4). Without
808	  the mode, no route is ever installed.
809	- **amnezia all-or-nothing.** An unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain
810	  WireGuard. Once *any* of `jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4` is set, the full
811	  `jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2` set must be `> 0`, `jmin <= jmax`, the init/response
812	  lengths must not collide (`148+s1 != 92+s2`), and `h1..h4` must be a distinct
813	  set (omitted headers default to `1..4`). The same profile must be set on both
814	  ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
815	- **`link_bandwidth` + `link_rtt` are a pair.** Both-or-neither, and
816	  **all-or-nothing across every path**: declare on every `[[paths]]` block or
817	  none (the shared pace is sized to the slowest declared link, undefined under a
818	  partial declaration). They take effect **only** under
819	  `scheduler.policy = "weighted"` with `pacing_enabled = true`; otherwise a
820	  declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are **mutually exclusive** with
821	  the raw `scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps` / `pacing_burst_frames` knobs —
822	  declare link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot knobs, not both.
823	- **scheduler off-unless-present.** No `[scheduler]` block ⇒ `active-backup` and
824	  every weighted knob ignored. Under `weighted`, omitted knobs take the defaults
825	  shown; the hysteresis band requires `disengage_fraction < engage_fraction`.
826	- **fec off-unless-present.** No `[fec]` block (or `enabled = false`) ⇒ no
827	  parity on the wire. `adaptive = true` requires `enabled = true`. In adaptive
828	  mode set **either** `target_residual` (recommended, `(0,1)`) **or**
829	  `safety_factor` (`>= 1`), never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
830	- **metrics loopback-only.** `[metrics] listen` must be a loopback address (or a
831	  hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
832	  the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.
833	
834	## 4. systemd units
835	
836	Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:
837	
838	```sh
839	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
840	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
841	systemctl daemon-reload
842	systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
843	```
844	
845	- `systemctl reload wanbond-<role>` sends SIGHUP: the daemon re-reads the
846	  config and applies the **path diff** (add/remove uplinks) without tearing
847	  down the live tunnel. A bad reload is logged and the running config kept.
848	- `Restart=on-failure` restarts the daemon if the tunnel engine tears down
849	  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
850	- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
851	  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
852	  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.
853	
854	### NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in
855	
856	On systems running **NetworkManager** (the default on RPi OS, Debian, and Ubuntu
857	desktop), NetworkManager monitors the `wanbond0` interface for link changes and
858	automatically flushes any configured IP addresses on link-up, destroying the
859	tunnel routing without operator action. This failure mode (D39, D5) is prevented
860	by marking `wanbond0` as an unmanaged device in NetworkManager's configuration.
861	
862	Deploy the shipped drop-in:
863	
864	```sh
865	cp packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/
866	```
867	
868	Then reload NetworkManager to apply the change:
869	
870	```sh
871	nmcli general reload
872	# or
873	sudo systemctl reload NetworkManager
874	```
875	
876	Verify the interface is marked unmanaged:
877	
878	```sh
879	nmcli device show wanbond0 | grep STATE
880	```
881	
882	The output should show `STATE: unmanaged`.
883	
884	If your edge box runs **systemd-networkd** instead, and NetworkManager is **not running**,
885	you do not need this drop-in — skip this subsection and proceed to "Interface
886	addressing and routing" below. (If NetworkManager is active alongside systemd-networkd,
887	the drop-in is still required to prevent the address flush.)
888	
889	### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)
890	
891	The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
892	administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
893	so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
894	but otherwise owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses**, and
895	**installs no routes** — with ONE narrow exception: a peer marked
896	`mode = "default-route"` (see §3) makes the daemon install that peer's
897	`allowed_ips` split (the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair for `0.0.0.0/0`) as scope-link
898	routes via `wanbond0`, and remove them on stop. That exception is routes ONLY —
899	no policy routing, SNAT, or concentrator forwarding, which remain operator-owned
900	(put them in the addressing script below). Addressing is always operator-owned (wg-quick
901	style, no privileged shell-outs); assign it with a **systemd-networkd `.network`
902	file**, using the inner addresses from `allowed_ips`:
903	
904	If something ELSE takes the link back down after boot (NetworkManager without
905	the unmanaged-devices drop-in below is the common case, D39), a `TUN write`
906	`EIO` no longer surfaces only as the engine's raw `input/output error`: the
907	daemon inspects the interface's link state and MTU and logs an actionable
908	ERROR naming the cause (e.g. `wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring it up
909	(install.md §4)`) alongside the raw errno, rate-limited to one diagnostic per
910	30s so a write storm does not flood the log.
911	
912	```ini
913	# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
914	[Match]
915	Name=wanbond0
916	
917	[Network]
918	Address=10.77.0.2/24
919	```
920	
921	```sh
922	systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd

file.content:
### 3z. Full configuration reference (all keys)

This is the exhaustive key list — every configuration key wanbond reads, in one
place. The example below is **edge-oriented**; concentrator-only keys are shown
and marked `CONCENTRATOR-ONLY`. Required keys are shown live; optional/defaulted
keys are shown **commented-out with their default value**. Uncommenting a key
and leaving it at the shown value is a no-op. The per-section notes after the
example capture the cross-field constraints a single example cannot express
inline. All of these are enforced at config load (`config.Load`), which fails
fast on the first violation — except the loopback and `allowed_ips` checks,
noted below.

> The same content ships as a copy-pasteable file at the repo root,
> [`wanbond.example.toml`](../wanbond.example.toml): copy it, fill the
> `<placeholders>`, and delete what you do not need.

```toml
# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
                                   #   inferred from other keys.
psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. Same value
                                   #   on both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. false => wanbond0 is
                                   #   destroyed on daemon stop (its addresses/
                                   #   routes/rules drop on every restart). true =>
                                   #   TUNSETPERSIST on start: wanbond0 survives a
                                   #   restart with the SAME ifindex, so operator-
                                   #   owned addressing persists. See "Interface
                                   #   addressing" below for the NM (D39) caveat.

# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
[[paths]]
name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's
                                   #   UDP socket binds to. Must be unique across
                                   #   paths (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE
                                   #   at the second bind). No default.
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the
                                   #   peer's endpoint is reused). No default.
                                   #   Inert on the concentrator (it learns edge
                                   #   endpoints from traffic).
# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT
                                   #   (Go duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when
                                   #   link_bandwidth is set under weighted
                                   #   pacing; ignored otherwise. No default.

# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
[[paths]]
name        = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"

# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there;
                                   #   omit / leave 0 on the edge. uint16
                                   #   (< 1024 needs CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).

# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key.
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`).
                                   #   ip:port literal (default) OR hostname
                                   #   with opt-in `dns = true` (§Optional
                                   #   [dns] resolver block). CONCENTRATOR:
                                   #   must be ABSENT (rejected) — it roams
                                   #   the edge dynamically. Mutually
                                   #   exclusive with `endpoints`.
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is
                                   #   the active concentrator, the rest ordered
                                   #   standbys. Each entry may be an ip:port
                                   #   literal or, with `dns = true`, a
                                   #   hostname; duplicates rejected within
                                   #   each form. Mutually exclusive with
                                   #   `endpoint` (which is its one-element
                                   #   form). Rejected on the concentrator.
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built). A
                                   #   literal 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 is always split
                                   #   into the equivalent /1+/1 pair at UAPI
                                   #   render.
# mode = "default-route"           # OPTIONAL, edge-only. Marks this peer as
                                   #   the edge's full-tunnel concentrator: the
                                   #   daemon installs this peer's allowed_ips
                                   #   split (wg-quick /1+/1 for 0.0.0.0/0) as
                                   #   routes via wanbond0 once the interface is
                                   #   up, and removes them on stop. Routes ONLY —
                                   #   no policy routing/SNAT/forwarding, which
                                   #   stay operator-owned (§4). Rejected on the
                                   #   concentrator.

# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire
# jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2 set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One engine per process.
# [amnezia]
# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must
                                   #   differ. (no defaults: required when block
                                   #   present.)
# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when
# h2 = 2                           #   the block is configured but NO header is
# h3 = 3                           #   given. When any is set, h1..h4 must be a
# h4 = 4                           #   distinct set (values <= 4 mean "standard
                                   #   message type").

# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
# [scheduler]
# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted".
                                   #   Every knob below applies ONLY to
                                   #   "weighted"; under active-backup they are
                                   #   inert and left unset.
# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate
                                   #   denominator and pacing refill rate. > 0.
# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below
                                   #   disengage_fraction * capacity. Must be in
                                   #   [0, engage_fraction) — the hysteresis band.
# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator
                                   #   time constant. > 0.
# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames).
                                   #   > 0 when pacing_enabled.
# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight
                                   #   formula. > 0.
# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in
                                   #   the weight formula. > 0.

# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
# [fec]
# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1
                                   #   when enabled. No default (required).
# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled;
                                   #   K + M <= 256. No default (required).
# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline.
                                   #   > 0 and <= 125ms.
# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1).
                                   #   No default. Mutually exclusive with
                                   #   safety_factor.
# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier
                                   #   >= 1. DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and
                                   #   NEITHER field is set. Must stay 0 in fixed
                                   #   mode. Mutually exclusive with
                                   #   target_residual.

# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8,
                                   #   ::1, or a hostname resolving only to
                                   #   loopback); any other address is REFUSED
                                   #   when the endpoint binds (not at load).

# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[log]
level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning" is
                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.
```

**Cross-field constraints and role applicability (not expressible inline):**

- **Roles.** `role` is required and never inferred. Edge-only:
  per-peer `endpoint`/`endpoints` (required on the edge, rejected on the
  concentrator) and the pacing declarations (`dest_addr` is edge-only in effect,
  but is not *rejected* on the concentrator — merely inert). Concentrator-only:
  `wireguard.listen_port` (required there, unused on the edge).
- **`endpoint` vs `endpoints`.** Mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the
  one-element form of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the
  concentrator must set neither. Each entry is an `ip:port` literal (default)
  or, with the peer's `dns = true` opt-in, a hostname resolved at runtime
  (never at config load); duplicates are rejected within each form
  (literal-vs-literal, hostname-vs-hostname) — see §Optional `[dns]` resolver
  block.
- **Distinct `source_addr`.** Each `[[paths]]` needs a unique, valid
  `source_addr` (compared unmapped, so `192.0.2.10` and `::ffff:192.0.2.10`
  collide). `name` must also be unique.
- **`allowed_ips`.** At least one CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG
  configuration is assembled (not by `config.validate`). A literal `0.0.0.0/0`
  or `::/0` (full tunnel) is ALWAYS split into the equivalent `/1`+`/1` pair at
  UAPI render — the engine never receives the literal `/0`, which wedges the
  handshake.
- **`mode` is edge-only.** Peer `mode = "default-route"` marks a peer as the
  edge's full-tunnel concentrator (an opt-in alongside a `0.0.0.0/0`/`::/0`
  `allowed_ips` entry); rejected on the concentrator, mirroring the
  `endpoint`/`dns` edge-only rules. When set, the daemon installs this peer's
  `allowed_ips` split (the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair for `0.0.0.0/0`) as scope-link
  routes via `wanbond0` once the interface is up, and withdraws them on stop —
  the daemon's ONLY route programming. It is deliberately minimal: routes ONLY,
  **no** client-LAN policy routing, SNAT, or concentrator `ip_forward`/
  `MASQUERADE`/`FORWARD` — those stay operator-owned recipes (see §4). Without
  the mode, no route is ever installed.
- **amnezia all-or-nothing.** An unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain
  WireGuard. Once *any* of `jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4` is set, the full
  `jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2` set must be `> 0`, `jmin <= jmax`, the init/response
  lengths must not collide (`148+s1 != 92+s2`), and `h1..h4` must be a distinct
  set (omitted headers default to `1..4`). The same profile must be set on both
  ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
- **`link_bandwidth` + `link_rtt` are a pair.** Both-or-neither, and
  **all-or-nothing across every path**: declare on every `[[paths]]` block or
  none (the shared pace is sized to the slowest declared link, undefined under a
  partial declaration). They take effect **only** under
  `scheduler.policy = "weighted"` with `pacing_enabled = true`; otherwise a
  declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are **mutually exclusive** with
  the raw `scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps` / `pacing_burst_frames` knobs —
  declare link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot knobs, not both.
- **scheduler off-unless-present.** No `[scheduler]` block ⇒ `active-backup` and
  every weighted knob ignored. Under `weighted`, omitted knobs take the defaults
  shown; the hysteresis band requires `disengage_fraction < engage_fraction`.
- **fec off-unless-present.** No `[fec]` block (or `enabled = false`) ⇒ no
  parity on the wire. `adaptive = true` requires `enabled = true`. In adaptive
  mode set **either** `target_residual` (recommended, `(0,1)`) **or**
  `safety_factor` (`>= 1`), never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
- **metrics loopback-only.** `[metrics] listen` must be a loopback address (or a
  hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
  the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.

## 4. systemd units

Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:

```sh
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
```

- `systemctl reload wanbond-<role>` sends SIGHUP: the daemon re-reads the
  config and applies the **path diff** (add/remove uplinks) without tearing
  down the live tunnel. A bad reload is logged and the running config kept.
- `Restart=on-failure` restarts the daemon if the tunnel engine tears down
  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.

### NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in

On systems running **NetworkManager** (the default on RPi OS, Debian, and Ubuntu
desktop), NetworkManager monitors the `wanbond0` interface for link changes and
automatically flushes any configured IP addresses on link-up, destroying the
tunnel routing without operator action. This failure mode (D39, D5) is prevented
by marking `wanbond0` as an unmanaged device in NetworkManager's configuration.

Deploy the shipped drop-in:

```sh
cp packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/
```

Then reload NetworkManager to apply the change:

```sh
nmcli general reload
# or
sudo systemctl reload NetworkManager
```

Verify the interface is marked unmanaged:

```sh
nmcli device show wanbond0 | grep STATE
```

The output should show `STATE: unmanaged`.

If your edge box runs **systemd-networkd** instead, and NetworkManager is **not running**,
you do not need this drop-in — skip this subsection and proceed to "Interface
addressing and routing" below. (If NetworkManager is active alongside systemd-networkd,
the drop-in is still required to prevent the address flush.)

### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)

The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
but otherwise owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses**, and
**installs no routes** — with ONE narrow exception: a peer marked
`mode = "default-route"` (see §3) makes the daemon install that peer's
`allowed_ips` split (the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair for `0.0.0.0/0`) as scope-link
routes via `wanbond0`, and remove them on stop. That exception is routes ONLY —
no policy routing, SNAT, or concentrator forwarding, which remain operator-owned
(put them in the addressing script below). Addressing is always operator-owned (wg-quick
style, no privileged shell-outs); assign it with a **systemd-networkd `.network`
file**, using the inner addresses from `allowed_ips`:

If something ELSE takes the link back down after boot (NetworkManager without
the unmanaged-devices drop-in below is the common case, D39), a `TUN write`
`EIO` no longer surfaces only as the engine's raw `input/output error`: the
daemon inspects the interface's link state and MTU and logs an actionable
ERROR naming the cause (e.g. `wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring it up
(install.md §4)`) alongside the raw errno, rate-limited to one diagnostic per
30s so a write storm does not flood the log.

```ini
# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
[Match]
Name=wanbond0

[Network]
Address=10.77.0.2/24
```

```sh
systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd

285	# [dns]
286	# resolver = "dot"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
287	# dot_server = "dns.example.com:853"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "dot"
288	# bootstrap_ip = "198.51.100.2"    # REQUIRED: dot_server's host is a hostname
289	                                   #   (BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: must reach the
290	                                   #   resolver WITHOUT DNS lookup itself).
291	# poll_interval = "30s"
292	# timeout = "5s"
293	#
294	# EXAMPLE 3 (system resolver, any IP-literal endpoint):
295	# [dns]
296	# resolver = "system"              # DEFAULT "system". No URL/server/bootstrap needed.
297	# poll_interval = "30s"
298	# timeout = "5s"
299	#
300	# Each EXAMPLE above is self-contained and independently valid: uncomment
301	# EXACTLY ONE (its [dns] header plus its fields) and nothing else. The three
302	# resolver modes are mutually exclusive — setting fields from more than one
303	# EXAMPLE at once (e.g. resolver = "doh" together with dot_server, or a
304	# bootstrap_ip on a doh_url/dot_server host that is already an IP literal) is
305	# rejected at config load. See internal/config/dns.go for every field's exact
306	# precondition.
307	
308	# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
309	[metrics]
310	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8, ::1, or
311	                                   #   a hostname resolving only to loopback); any
312	                                   #   other address is REFUSED when the endpoint
313	                                   #   binds (not at load).
314	
315	# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
316	[log]
317	level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
318	                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning"
319	                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.
320	
321	# ── Cross-field constraints (not expressible inline) ─────────────────────────
322	# • Roles: `role` is required, never inferred. Edge-only: per-peer endpoint/
323	#   endpoints (required on edge, rejected on concentrator) + the pacing
324	#   declarations (dest_addr is edge-only in effect, merely inert on the
325	#   concentrator). Concentrator-only: wireguard.listen_port (required there).
326	# • endpoint vs endpoints: mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the one-element form
327	#   of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the concentrator sets
328	#   neither. Each entry is an ip:port literal (default) or, with the peer's
329	#   `dns = true` opt-in, a hostname:port resolved at runtime (never at config
330	#   load). Duplicates are rejected within each form (literal-vs-literal,
331	#   hostname:port-vs-hostname:port) — a hostname is never compared against a
332	#   literal at load time.
333	# • Distinct source_addr: each [[paths]] needs a unique, valid source_addr
334	#   (compared unmapped, so 192.0.2.10 and ::ffff:192.0.2.10 collide). `name` must
335	#   also be unique.
336	# • allowed_ips: >= 1 CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG config is
337	#   assembled (not by config.validate).
338	# • amnezia all-or-nothing: an unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain WireGuard.
339	#   Once ANY of jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4 is set, the full jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2 set
340	#   must be > 0, jmin <= jmax, 148+s1 != 92+s2, and h1..h4 must be a distinct set
341	#   (omitted headers default to 1..4). Same profile on both ends. One engine/process.
342	# • link_bandwidth + link_rtt are a pair: both-or-neither, and all-or-nothing
343	#   across every path (declare on every [[paths]] block or none). They take effect
344	#   ONLY under scheduler.policy = "weighted" with pacing_enabled = true; otherwise
345	#   a declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are mutually exclusive with the
346	#   raw per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames knobs.
347	# • scheduler off-unless-present: no [scheduler] => active-backup, every weighted
348	#   knob ignored. Under weighted, the hysteresis band requires
349	#   disengage_fraction < engage_fraction.
350	# • fec off-unless-present: no [fec] (or enabled = false) => no parity. adaptive =
351	#   true requires enabled = true. In adaptive mode set EITHER target_residual OR
352	#   safety_factor, never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
353	# • metrics loopback-only: listen must be a loopback address (or a hostname
354	#   resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when the
355	#   endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics.
356	# • dns off-unless-present: no [dns] => system resolver, default cadence/
357	#   timeouts; [dns] only selects the transport a peer's `dns = true` opt-in
358	#   uses, never a gate by itself. doh_url/dot_server are mode-specific: set
359	#   exactly the one matching `resolver`, the other (and bootstrap_ip under
360	#   resolver = "system") must stay absent. BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: a
361	#   hostname-form doh_url/dot_server host REQUIRES bootstrap_ip; an IP
362	#   literal host REJECTS a non-empty bootstrap_ip (mode mismatch).
363
