# wanbond.example.toml — exhaustive annotated configuration reference.
#
# Every key wanbond reads is present here. Copy this file, fill the <placeholders>,
# and delete what you do not need:
#
#   sudo install -m 0600 /dev/null /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/edge.toml     # paste + edit this content
#
# 0600 is MANDATORY: the file holds the WireGuard private key and the PSK, so
# config.Load refuses any file whose mode is not exactly 0600.
#
# This example is EDGE-oriented. For a CONCENTRATOR: set role = "concentrator",
# uncomment [wireguard].listen_port, and REMOVE the peer `endpoint`/`endpoints`
# (the concentrator learns the edge's endpoints from authenticated traffic).
#
# Required keys are shown live; optional keys are commented-out with their
# DEFAULT value (uncommenting one at its shown value is a no-op). Cross-field
# constraints a single example cannot express inline are listed at the bottom.
# Full prose reference: docs/install.md §3z. Everything here is enforced at
# config load (fail-fast on the first violation) except the loopback + allowed_ips
# checks noted below.

# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
                                   #   inferred from other keys.
psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. SAME value on
                                   #   both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. When false the TUN
                                   #   wanbond0 is destroyed on daemon stop, so its
                                   #   addresses/routes/rules are dropped on every
                                   #   restart (today's teardown semantics). Set
                                   #   true and the daemon issues TUNSETPERSIST on
                                   #   start: wanbond0 SURVIVES a daemon restart
                                   #   with the SAME ifindex, so operator-owned
                                   #   addressing persists untouched. NOTE (D39): a
                                   #   persistent device STILL needs the
                                   #   NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in on
                                   #   NM hosts — persistence keeps the link, it
                                   #   does not exempt it from NM.

# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
[[paths]]
name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's UDP
                                   #   socket binds to. Must be unique across paths
                                   #   (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the
                                   #   second bind). No default.
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the peer's
                                   #   endpoint is reused). No default. Inert on the
                                   #   concentrator (it learns edge endpoints).
# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT (Go
                                   #   duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when link_bandwidth
                                   #   is set under weighted pacing; else ignored.

# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
[[paths]]
name        = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"

# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
                                   #   Generate: wg genkey (public: … | wg pubkey)
# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there; omit /
                                   #   leave 0 on the edge. uint16 (< 1024 needs
                                   #   CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).

# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key (the OTHER
                                   #   end's public key).
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`). ip:port
                                   #   literal (default) OR hostname with opt-in
                                   #   `dns = true` below. CONCENTRATOR: must be
                                   #   ABSENT (rejected) — it roams the edge
                                   #   dynamically. Mutually exclusive with
                                   #   `endpoints`.
# dns = false                       # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. Set true to enable
                                   #   hostname resolution for the endpoint above.
                                   #   The [dns] block below is OPTIONAL: it only
                                   #   selects the resolver TRANSPORT and defaults
                                   #   to the system resolver when absent.
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is the
                                   #   active concentrator, the rest ordered
                                   #   standbys. Each may be an ip:port literal
                                   #   (default) or a hostname with dns = true; when
                                   #   hostnames are used, each resolves to its full
                                   #   A+AAAA set and all addresses are ordered
                                   #   together in the failover list. duplicates
                                   #   rejected. Mutually exclusive with `endpoint`
                                   #   (its one-element form). Rejected on the
                                   #   concentrator.
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built). This is
                                   #   the peer's inner tunnel address(es). A literal
                                   #   0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 (full tunnel) is ALWAYS split
                                   #   into the equivalent /1+/1 pair at UAPI render —
                                   #   the engine never sees the literal /0.
# mode = "default-route"           # OPTIONAL, edge-only. Marks this peer as the
                                   #   edge's full-tunnel concentrator (opts into a
                                   #   0.0.0.0/0 / ::/0 allowed_ips entry above).
                                   #   Rejected on the concentrator. When set, the
                                   #   daemon installs this peer's allowed_ips split
                                   #   (wg-quick /1+/1 for 0.0.0.0/0) as scope-link
                                   #   routes via wanbond0 once the interface is up,
                                   #   and removes them on stop. Routes ONLY — no
                                   #   policy routing, SNAT, or concentrator
                                   #   forwarding (those stay operator-owned).
# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # OPTIONAL. Meaningful only for a MULTI-PEER
                                   #   concentrator (see the example below). With
                                   #   exactly one peer configured — as shown live
                                   #   here — a per-peer psk is REJECTED at load
                                   #   ("psk is not meaningful with a single peer");
                                   #   leave it UNSET and the top-level `psk` above
                                   #   is used, so a legacy single-peer config is
                                   #   unaffected. With MORE THAN ONE peer, this
                                   #   field becomes REQUIRED and must be pairwise-
                                   #   distinct across peers: each peer authenticates
                                   #   its own PROBE frames with its OWN psk, and the
                                   #   top-level `psk` authenticates NO peer once a
                                   #   second peer is configured (T80/T81, Q21).
                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
# name = "edge1"                    # OPTIONAL. Unused when a single peer is
                                   #   configured, as shown live here (metrics never
                                   #   carry a `peer` label for a single bound peer,
                                   #   see docs/runbook.md's "Multi-peer concentrator
                                   #   (G4)" metrics note). With MORE THAN ONE peer,
                                   #   REQUIRED and must be unique across peers
                                   #   (T80/T81, Q21). ADDITIONAL peers (all but the
                                   #   first-configured) are exposed under this name
                                   #   as the metrics `peer` label; the first/primary
                                   #   peer's series always carry the empty label
                                   #   peer="" regardless of this field (D58).

# ── second peer example (hostname endpoint with DNS opt-in) ──────────────────────
# [[wireguard.peers]]
# public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"
# endpoint = "concentrator.example.com:51820"  # hostname endpoint (requires dns = true)
# dns = true                        # enable hostname resolution for this endpoint
#                                   #   ([dns] block below is OPTIONAL — absent
#                                   #   means the system resolver; see [dns])
# allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# MULTI-PEER CONCENTRATOR EXAMPLE (G4): TWO EDGES, ONE CONCENTRATOR
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# The plural [[wireguard.peers]] syntax (one block per edge) is supported on the
# concentrator. Single-peer configs (one [[wireguard.peers]] block, as shown
# live above) are unaffected: with exactly one peer, a per-peer `psk` is
# REJECTED at load (the top-level `psk` is used) and a per-peer `name` is
# optional and inert for metrics.
#
# With MORE THAN ONE peer, every peer authenticates its own PROBE frames with
# its OWN per-peer `psk` (internal/bind/multipath.go's peerBySource demux binds
# a source only once a PROBE MAC-verifies under that peer's psk); the
# top-level `psk` below remains REQUIRED by config validation but
# authenticates NO peer once a second peer is configured
# (internal/device/device.go feeds only per-peer PSKs to the bind — see
# docs/design.md's Security model). Each peer's `name` is REQUIRED and must be
# unique; ADDITIONAL peers (all but the first-configured) are exposed under
# their configured name as the metrics `peer` label, while the first/primary
# peer's series still carry the empty label peer="" — byte-compatible with the
# single-peer exposition, but a discrepancy versus the "name required" config
# rule filed as defect D58 (documented current behavior; not fixed here).
#
# ── live example (two edges, distinct per-peer psk/name) ─────────────────────
# role = "concentrator"
# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED by validate; authenticates no
                                   #   peer once a second peer is configured.
#
# [[paths]]
# name = "wan0"
# source_addr = "10.0.0.5"
#
# [wireguard]
# private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"
# listen_port = 51820
#
# # Edge 1: office — bound FIRST, so its metrics series carry peer="" (D58)
# # even though `name` is set and required in multi-peer mode.
# [[wireguard.peers]]
# public_key = "<base64 office-edge public key>"
# allowed_ips = ["10.77.1.0/24"]
# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for office-edge>"    # REQUIRED in multi-peer mode
# name = "office-edge"                             # REQUIRED in multi-peer mode
#
# # Edge 2: mobile — registered via AddConcentratorPeer, so its metrics
# # series carry peer="mobile-edge".
# [[wireguard.peers]]
# public_key = "<base64 mobile-edge public key>"
# allowed_ips = ["10.77.2.0/24"]
# psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for mobile-edge>"    # Must differ from office-edge's psk
# name = "mobile-edge"                             # Must differ from office-edge's name
#
# [metrics]
# listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"

# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2
# set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
# [amnezia]
# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must differ.
                                   #   (no defaults: required when the block present.)
# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when the
# h2 = 2                           #   block is configured but NO header is given.
# h3 = 3                           #   When any is set, h1..h4 must be a DISTINCT set
# h4 = 4                           #   (values <= 4 mean "standard message type").

# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
# [scheduler]
# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted". Every
                                   #   knob below applies ONLY to "weighted"; under
                                   #   active-backup they are inert and left unset.
# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate denominator
                                   #   and pacing refill rate. > 0.
# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below disengage_fraction
                                   #   * capacity. Must be in [0, engage_fraction) —
                                   #   the hysteresis band.
# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator time
                                   #   constant. > 0.
# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames). > 0
                                   #   when pacing_enabled.
# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight formula. >0.
# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in the
                                   #   weight formula. > 0.

# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
# [fec]
# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1 when
                                   #   enabled. No default (required).
# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled; K + M <=
                                   #   256. No default (required).
# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline. > 0
                                   #   and <= 125ms.
# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1). No
                                   #   default. Mutually exclusive with safety_factor.
# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier >= 1.
                                   #   DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and NEITHER field
                                   #   is set. Must stay 0 in fixed mode. Mutually
                                   #   exclusive with target_residual.

# ── dns: OPTIONAL. Omitted => system resolver, default cadence/timeouts ───────
# Selects the TRANSPORT a peer's opt-in hostname endpoint (dns = true, above)
# is resolved through. [dns] alone never turns hostname resolution on.
#
# EXAMPLE 1 (IP-literal DoH resolver):
# [dns]
# resolver = "doh"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
# doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "doh"
# poll_interval = "30s"            # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT 30s. Re-resolution cadence.
# timeout = "5s"                   # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT 5s. Per-lookup timeout.
#
# EXAMPLE 2 (hostname DoT resolver, requires bootstrap_ip):
# [dns]
# resolver = "dot"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
# dot_server = "dns.example.com:853"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "dot"
# bootstrap_ip = "198.51.100.2"    # REQUIRED: dot_server's host is a hostname
                                   #   (BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: must reach the
                                   #   resolver WITHOUT DNS lookup itself).
# poll_interval = "30s"
# timeout = "5s"
#
# EXAMPLE 3 (system resolver, any IP-literal endpoint):
# [dns]
# resolver = "system"              # DEFAULT "system". No URL/server/bootstrap needed.
# poll_interval = "30s"
# timeout = "5s"
#
# Each EXAMPLE above is self-contained and independently valid: uncomment
# EXACTLY ONE (its [dns] header plus its fields) and nothing else. The three
# resolver modes are mutually exclusive — setting fields from more than one
# EXAMPLE at once (e.g. resolver = "doh" together with dot_server, or a
# bootstrap_ip on a doh_url/dot_server host that is already an IP literal) is
# rejected at config load. See internal/config/dns.go for every field's exact
# precondition.

# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8, ::1, or
                                   #   a hostname resolving only to loopback); any
                                   #   other address is REFUSED when the endpoint
                                   #   binds (not at load).

# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[log]
level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning"
                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.

# ── Cross-field constraints (not expressible inline) ─────────────────────────
# • Roles: `role` is required, never inferred. Edge-only: per-peer endpoint/
#   endpoints (required on edge, rejected on concentrator) + the pacing
#   declarations (dest_addr is edge-only in effect, merely inert on the
#   concentrator). Concentrator-only: wireguard.listen_port (required there).
# • endpoint vs endpoints: mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the one-element form
#   of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the concentrator sets
#   neither. Each entry is an ip:port literal (default) or, with the peer's
#   `dns = true` opt-in, a hostname:port resolved at runtime (never at config
#   load). Duplicates are rejected within each form (literal-vs-literal,
#   hostname:port-vs-hostname:port) — a hostname is never compared against a
#   literal at load time.
# • Distinct source_addr: each [[paths]] needs a unique, valid source_addr
#   (compared unmapped, so 192.0.2.10 and ::ffff:192.0.2.10 collide). `name` must
#   also be unique.
# • allowed_ips: >= 1 CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG config is
#   assembled (not by config.validate).
# • amnezia all-or-nothing: an unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain WireGuard.
#   Once ANY of jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4 is set, the full jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2 set
#   must be > 0, jmin <= jmax, 148+s1 != 92+s2, and h1..h4 must be a distinct set
#   (omitted headers default to 1..4). Same profile on both ends. One engine/process.
# • link_bandwidth + link_rtt are a pair: both-or-neither, and all-or-nothing
#   across every path (declare on every [[paths]] block or none). They take effect
#   ONLY under scheduler.policy = "weighted" with pacing_enabled = true; otherwise
#   a declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are mutually exclusive with the
#   raw per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames knobs.
# • scheduler off-unless-present: no [scheduler] => active-backup, every weighted
#   knob ignored. Under weighted, the hysteresis band requires
#   disengage_fraction < engage_fraction.
# • fec off-unless-present: no [fec] (or enabled = false) => no parity. adaptive =
#   true requires enabled = true. In adaptive mode set EITHER target_residual OR
#   safety_factor, never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
# • metrics loopback-only: listen must be a loopback address (or a hostname
#   resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when the
#   endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics.
# • dns off-unless-present: no [dns] => system resolver, default cadence/
#   timeouts; [dns] only selects the transport a peer's `dns = true` opt-in
#   uses, never a gate by itself. doh_url/dot_server are mode-specific: set
#   exactly the one matching `resolver`, the other (and bootstrap_ip under
#   resolver = "system") must stay absent. BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: a
#   hostname-form doh_url/dot_server host REQUIRES bootstrap_ip; an IP

content.content:
576	## Load-bearing invariants
577	
578	These are the rules that keep the design correct; break them and the tunnel
579	misbehaves subtly. Agents and contributors must preserve them.
580	
581	1. **One virtual endpoint per peer (A1).** The engine sees a single stable
582	   `Endpoint`; the Bind fans out beneath it. Never surface per-path endpoint
583	   churn to the engine.
584	2. **Own outer sequence space.** The resequencer/FEC use wanbond's outer-seq;
585	   never reuse or perturb the inner WireGuard counter.
586	3. **Resequence before inner anti-replay.** Restore cross-path order in `reseq`
587	   before the engine's replay window validates.
588	4. **Inner fail-closed; outer control authenticated.** WireGuard authenticates the
589	   payload; PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay;
590	   DATA/PARITY are deliberately unauthenticated (see Security model).
591	5. **Amnezia is isolated to `bind.go`.** All engine coupling goes through the type
592	   aliases there.
593	6. **Amnezia is all-or-nothing** and **single-engine-per-process** (its magic-
594	   header logic uses package-level globals). Config validation enforces the
595	   former; deployments must honor the latter.
596	7. **Re-verify the reedsolomon prefix invariant** on any FEC-dependency bump.
597	
598	## Security model
599	
600	- **Payload**: confidentiality, integrity, authenticity provided by inner
601	  WireGuard (Noise + AEAD). wanbond never sees plaintext.
602	- **Outer control plane** (PROBE, CONTROL): PSK-HMAC authenticated + per-peer
603	  monotonic anti-replay — an attacker cannot forge or replay them.
604	  - **Per-peer PSK (multi-peer concentrator, G4):** on a concentrator with more
605	    than one configured peer, each edge authenticates PROBE frames with its OWN
606	    per-peer `psk` — this field is REQUIRED and must be pairwise-distinct across
607	    peers (config load rejects a duplicate). The concentrator uses PROBE
608	    MAC-verification to learn each source address's owning peer (`peerBySource`
609	    binding in `internal/bind/multipath.go`); a source that MAC-verifies under
610	    peer A's psk is bound to A, and subsequent DATA/PARITY frames from it route
611	    to A without re-authentication. The top-level `psk` remains REQUIRED by
612	    config validation in every configuration, but on a multi-peer concentrator it
613	    authenticates **no peer**: `device.Up` feeds only each peer's own PSK (from
614	    `Config.PeerIdentities`) into the bind, so an existing single-peer edge does
615	    NOT keep authenticating via the top-level psk once a second peer is added —
616	    it must be given its own per-peer psk at that point. With a single configured
617	    peer, a per-peer `psk` is instead REJECTED at config load (not merely
618	    defaulted) and the top-level `psk` is the sole authenticator, so existing
619	    single-peer deployments parse and run identically unchanged.
620	- **Outer data plane** (DATA, PARITY): **unauthenticated by design**. A network
621	  attacker can forge/replay these; forgeries are dropped by the inner AEAD (real
622	  payload) or discarded by the FEC decoder as inconsistent. The accepted residual
623	  risk is **DoS-grade only** (an attacker can waste decode/resequence work), never
624	  a confidentiality or integrity break. This trade buys ~0 per-packet overhead on
625	  the hot path.
626	  - **In multi-peer mode**, unauthenticated DATA/PARITY do not establish
627	    source-to-peer bindings (they route using existing bindings learned from
628	    PROBE frames). The two forgery cases cost differently: a forged source
629	    address with **no existing binding** is trial-decoded against each
630	    configured peer's codec (`O(peers)`, bounded by the static peer count,
631	    `demuxInbound`) and, carrying no PROBE MAC, is dropped there — it never
632	    dispatches to a peer and never reaches that peer's resequencer or FEC
633	    decoder. A forged source that spoofs an address **already bound** to a
634	    peer (learned from that peer's authenticated PROBE) routes straight into
635	    that peer's plane and wastes its resequence/FEC work — the same
636	    DoS-grade residual described above for the single-peer case — before the
637	    inner AEAD rejects the forged payload. Neither case lets an attacker
638	    impersonate an existing peer: bindings are established and re-pointed
639	    only by an authenticated PROBE.
640	- **Traffic analysis / DPI**: the outer wire has no fingerprint (random nonce,
641	  obfuscated body, no magic bytes); AmneziaWG junk params add defense-in-depth.
642	  Protocol *mimicry* (looking like HTTPS) is an explicit non-goal.
643	
644	## Not yet built / deliberate boundaries
645	
646	These are recorded design boundaries, not defects:
647	
648	- **No live CONTROL protocol.** The CONTROL frame kind and its `ControlGuard`
649	  anti-replay exist and are tested, but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped at
650	  the Bind (`multipath.go` receive default case). It is the chokepoint a future
651	  out-of-band signalling layer (e.g. explicit rekey/state) must route through.
652	- **Pacing ships disabled by default; no live auto-tuning (Q20).** Empirical
653	  sizing *is* built: `SizePacingFromBDP` derives the per-path pace from an
654	  operator-declared per-link bandwidth (`link_bandwidth`/`link_rtt`) at config
655	  load (T53), and enabled-pacing was measured to eliminate bufferbloat both on the
656	  bandwidth-capped netns fixture (T56/T61) and on the real-link tier (T58). What
657	  stays a **deliberate boundary**: pacing is off unless `[scheduler] pacing_enabled
658	  = true`, and the declared bandwidth is fixed at load — wanbond never re-derives
659	  the pace live from runtime measurements (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the
660	  pilot). Absent a declared bandwidth (or with pacing off) the default per-path
661	  capacity is synthetic (~115 Mbit/s), well above realistic uplinks.
662	- **In-fixture throughput/bufferbloat measurement is CPU-bound (a fixture
663	  boundary).** The netns fixture proves *functional* bonding/FEC/failover/DPI but
664	  is CPU/PPS-bound, so absolute "bonded ≈ sum of links" throughput and bufferbloat
665	  are **not** measured there — they are measured on the **real-link tier**. The
666	  capped-fixture BDP sub-test (T52) and the realhosts tier (`just p0-baseline` →
667	  `TestRealAggregationBufferbloat` / `TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T58/T63) record
668	  the aggregation ratio and loaded-vs-idle RTT **report-only**. Note the realhosts
669	  topology shares a single physical uplink, so the measured aggregation ratio is
670	  ~≤1 — this is an informational, report-only measurement, not a bandwidth-
671	  aggregation guarantee (see
672	  [manual-checklist.md §P0](manual-checklist.md#p0--automated-real-link-baseline-realhosts-tier)
673	  and [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md)).
674	- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: UDP-only remains a non-goal.** Q18 brought
675	  edge-side ORDERED-ENDPOINT ACTIVE-STANDBY hub failover into scope; the config
676	  surface (T54), the switch (T57), the netns e2e (T62), and the real-link
677	  mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63) are all built and validated (see *Concentrator
678	  hub failover* above). What stays out of scope: UDP-only is deliberate — there is
679	  no TCP/TLS fallback for wholesale-UDP-block networks. The endpoint list itself
680	  is no longer IP:port-only: an entry may also be a hostname behind the peer's
681	  `dns = true` opt-in (see *DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs* above).
682	
683	## References
684	
685	- [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md) — the P0 spike that fixed the single-endpoint,
686	  resequencing, and fixture-is-CPU-bound decisions.
687	- [p0-checkpoint.md](p0-checkpoint.md) — phase gate + deferred-work record.
688	- [install.md](install.md) — deployment and operation.
689	- [manual-checklist.md](manual-checklist.md) — manual per-phase + real-link
690	  verification.

## Load-bearing invariants

These are the rules that keep the design correct; break them and the tunnel
misbehaves subtly. Agents and contributors must preserve them.

1. **One virtual endpoint per peer (A1).** The engine sees a single stable
   `Endpoint`; the Bind fans out beneath it. Never surface per-path endpoint
   churn to the engine.
2. **Own outer sequence space.** The resequencer/FEC use wanbond's outer-seq;
   never reuse or perturb the inner WireGuard counter.
3. **Resequence before inner anti-replay.** Restore cross-path order in `reseq`
   before the engine's replay window validates.
4. **Inner fail-closed; outer control authenticated.** WireGuard authenticates the
   payload; PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay;
   DATA/PARITY are deliberately unauthenticated (see Security model).
5. **Amnezia is isolated to `bind.go`.** All engine coupling goes through the type
   aliases there.
6. **Amnezia is all-or-nothing** and **single-engine-per-process** (its magic-
   header logic uses package-level globals). Config validation enforces the
   former; deployments must honor the latter.
7. **Re-verify the reedsolomon prefix invariant** on any FEC-dependency bump.

## Security model

- **Payload**: confidentiality, integrity, authenticity provided by inner
  WireGuard (Noise + AEAD). wanbond never sees plaintext.
- **Outer control plane** (PROBE, CONTROL): PSK-HMAC authenticated + per-peer
  monotonic anti-replay — an attacker cannot forge or replay them.
  - **Per-peer PSK (multi-peer concentrator, G4):** on a concentrator with more
    than one configured peer, each edge authenticates PROBE frames with its OWN
    per-peer `psk` — this field is REQUIRED and must be pairwise-distinct across
    peers (config load rejects a duplicate). The concentrator uses PROBE
    MAC-verification to learn each source address's owning peer (`peerBySource`
    binding in `internal/bind/multipath.go`); a source that MAC-verifies under
    peer A's psk is bound to A, and subsequent DATA/PARITY frames from it route
    to A without re-authentication. The top-level `psk` remains REQUIRED by
    config validation in every configuration, but on a multi-peer concentrator it
    authenticates **no peer**: `device.Up` feeds only each peer's own PSK (from
    `Config.PeerIdentities`) into the bind, so an existing single-peer edge does
    NOT keep authenticating via the top-level psk once a second peer is added —
    it must be given its own per-peer psk at that point. With a single configured
    peer, a per-peer `psk` is instead REJECTED at config load (not merely
    defaulted) and the top-level `psk` is the sole authenticator, so existing
    single-peer deployments parse and run identically unchanged.
- **Outer data plane** (DATA, PARITY): **unauthenticated by design**. A network
  attacker can forge/replay these; forgeries are dropped by the inner AEAD (real
  payload) or discarded by the FEC decoder as inconsistent. The accepted residual
  risk is **DoS-grade only** (an attacker can waste decode/resequence work), never
  a confidentiality or integrity break. This trade buys ~0 per-packet overhead on
  the hot path.
  - **In multi-peer mode**, unauthenticated DATA/PARITY do not establish
    source-to-peer bindings (they route using existing bindings learned from
    PROBE frames). The two forgery cases cost differently: a forged source
    address with **no existing binding** is trial-decoded against each
    configured peer's codec (`O(peers)`, bounded by the static peer count,
    `demuxInbound`) and, carrying no PROBE MAC, is dropped there — it never
    dispatches to a peer and never reaches that peer's resequencer or FEC
    decoder. A forged source that spoofs an address **already bound** to a
    peer (learned from that peer's authenticated PROBE) routes straight into
    that peer's plane and wastes its resequence/FEC work — the same
    DoS-grade residual described above for the single-peer case — before the
    inner AEAD rejects the forged payload. Neither case lets an attacker
    impersonate an existing peer: bindings are established and re-pointed
    only by an authenticated PROBE.
- **Traffic analysis / DPI**: the outer wire has no fingerprint (random nonce,
  obfuscated body, no magic bytes); AmneziaWG junk params add defense-in-depth.
  Protocol *mimicry* (looking like HTTPS) is an explicit non-goal.

## Not yet built / deliberate boundaries

These are recorded design boundaries, not defects:

- **No live CONTROL protocol.** The CONTROL frame kind and its `ControlGuard`
  anti-replay exist and are tested, but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped at
  the Bind (`multipath.go` receive default case). It is the chokepoint a future
  out-of-band signalling layer (e.g. explicit rekey/state) must route through.
- **Pacing ships disabled by default; no live auto-tuning (Q20).** Empirical
  sizing *is* built: `SizePacingFromBDP` derives the per-path pace from an
  operator-declared per-link bandwidth (`link_bandwidth`/`link_rtt`) at config
  load (T53), and enabled-pacing was measured to eliminate bufferbloat both on the
  bandwidth-capped netns fixture (T56/T61) and on the real-link tier (T58). What
  stays a **deliberate boundary**: pacing is off unless `[scheduler] pacing_enabled
  = true`, and the declared bandwidth is fixed at load — wanbond never re-derives
  the pace live from runtime measurements (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the
  pilot). Absent a declared bandwidth (or with pacing off) the default per-path
  capacity is synthetic (~115 Mbit/s), well above realistic uplinks.
- **In-fixture throughput/bufferbloat measurement is CPU-bound (a fixture
  boundary).** The netns fixture proves *functional* bonding/FEC/failover/DPI but
  is CPU/PPS-bound, so absolute "bonded ≈ sum of links" throughput and bufferbloat
  are **not** measured there — they are measured on the **real-link tier**. The
  capped-fixture BDP sub-test (T52) and the realhosts tier (`just p0-baseline` →
  `TestRealAggregationBufferbloat` / `TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T58/T63) record
  the aggregation ratio and loaded-vs-idle RTT **report-only**. Note the realhosts
  topology shares a single physical uplink, so the measured aggregation ratio is
  ~≤1 — this is an informational, report-only measurement, not a bandwidth-
  aggregation guarantee (see
  [manual-checklist.md §P0](manual-checklist.md#p0--automated-real-link-baseline-realhosts-tier)
  and [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md)).
- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: UDP-only remains a non-goal.** Q18 brought
  edge-side ORDERED-ENDPOINT ACTIVE-STANDBY hub failover into scope; the config
  surface (T54), the switch (T57), the netns e2e (T62), and the real-link
  mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63) are all built and validated (see *Concentrator
  hub failover* above). What stays out of scope: UDP-only is deliberate — there is
  no TCP/TLS fallback for wholesale-UDP-block networks. The endpoint list itself
  is no longer IP:port-only: an entry may also be a hostname behind the peer's
  `dns = true` opt-in (see *DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs* above).

## References

- [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md) — the P0 spike that fixed the single-endpoint,
  resequencing, and fixture-is-CPU-bound decisions.
- [p0-checkpoint.md](p0-checkpoint.md) — phase gate + deferred-work record.
- [install.md](install.md) — deployment and operation.
- [manual-checklist.md](manual-checklist.md) — manual per-phase + real-link
  verification.

content.content:
1	# wanbond design
2	
3	This document explains wanbond's architecture and, specifically, **exactly what
4	we built on top of the amneziawg-go WireGuard engine**. For setup and operation
5	see [install.md](install.md); for the front-door overview see the
6	[README](../README.md).
7	
8	## Thesis
9	
10	> Keep the WireGuard engine **unmodified**. Put **all** bonding logic in a custom
11	> `conn.Bind` beneath it, operating only on opaque, already-encrypted datagrams.
12	
13	We embed [amneziawg-go](https://github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go) as a library
14	and use it exactly as intended — for TUN management, the Noise handshake, AEAD
15	encryption, key rotation (rekey), endpoint roaming, and keepalives. We add
16	**nothing** inside the engine. Everything wanbond does — multipath scheduling,
17	outer-frame obfuscation, forward error correction, receive resequencing, and
18	per-path telemetry — lives in an implementation of the engine's `conn.Bind`
19	transport interface, which the engine drives for every packet it sends and
20	receives.
21	
22	This gives a clean separation: WireGuard owns confidentiality, integrity, and
23	authenticity of the *payload*; wanbond owns *delivery* across multiple lossy
24	paths, plus outer obfuscation. The Bind never inspects plaintext — it moves
25	opaque ciphertext datagrams.
26	
27	## Why amneziawg-go (and not plain wireguard-go)
28	
29	The architecture decision is closed: amneziawg-go over plain wireguard-go,
30	kcp-go, or quic-go. The reason is **DPI resistance** (requirement 6). AmneziaWG
31	adds configurable obfuscation to the WireGuard wire — junk packets (`jc`, `jmin`,
32	`jmax`), handshake junk prefixes (`s1`, `s2`), and custom magic headers
33	(`h1`–`h4`) — as *defense-in-depth* beneath our own outer obfuscation codec.
34	Using the engine as-is means WireGuard's battle-tested crypto and roaming come
35	for free while the obfuscation knobs are available when configured.
36	
37	**Fork-lag hedge.** amneziawg-go is a fork of wireguard-go and can lag upstream
38	security/perf fixes. We contain that risk: the entire dependency on the engine's
39	`conn` package is isolated to **one file**, `internal/bind/bind.go`, via type
40	aliases (`Bind = conn.Bind`, `Endpoint = conn.Endpoint`,
41	`ReceiveFunc = conn.ReceiveFunc`). The `conn.Bind`/`conn.Endpoint` contracts are
42	byte-identical between the two forks, so swapping back to upstream wireguard-go
43	(dropping obfuscation) touches only that file.
44	
45	## The data path
46	
47	```
48	                          EDGE                                     CONCENTRATOR
49	   ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐      ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
50	   │  applications / kernel routing         │      │       kernel routing / NAT onward      │
51	   │                 │                       │      │                 ▲                       │
52	   │           ┌─────▼──────┐  TUN           │      │           ┌─────┴──────┐  TUN           │
53	   │           │ WireGuard  │  (amneziawg-go)│      │           │ WireGuard  │                │
54	   │           │  engine    │  Noise/AEAD    │      │           │  engine    │                │
55	   │           └─────┬──────┘  rekey/roam    │      │           └─────▲──────┘                │
56	   │   opaque encrypted datagrams  │         │      │       opaque encrypted datagrams        │
57	   │           ┌─────▼──────────────────────┐│      │┌──────────────────────┴─────┐          │
58	   │           │   wanbond conn.Bind        ││      ││   wanbond conn.Bind        │          │
59	   │           │  ┌───────────────────────┐ ││      ││  ┌───────────────────────┐ │          │
60	   │  send ───►│  │ scheduler (sched)     │ ││      ││  │ resequencer (reseq)   │ │──► recv  │
61	   │           │  │ FEC encode (fec)      │ ││      ││  │ FEC decode (fec)      │ │          │
62	   │           │  │ frame codec (frame)   │ ││      ││  │ frame codec (frame)   │ │          │
63	   │           │  └──────────┬────────────┘ ││      ││  └──────────▲────────────┘ │          │
64	   │           │   per-path UDP sockets      ││      ││   per-path UDP sockets     │          │
65	   │           └──────┬───────────┬─────────┘│      │└──────▲───────────▲─────────┘          │
66	   └──────────────────┼───────────┼──────────┘      └───────┼───────────┼──────────────────┘
67	              starlink│    cellular│   ══════ real internet ══════│  path A    │ path B
68	```
69	
70	**Send** (edge → concentrator): the engine hands the Bind an opaque encrypted
71	datagram → the **scheduler** picks the path(s) → **FEC** optionally emits parity
72	frames for the group → the **frame codec** wraps each datagram in an obfuscated
73	outer frame → it goes out the chosen per-path UDP socket.
74	
75	**Receive** (concentrator side): frames arrive on the per-path sockets → the
76	**frame codec** de-obfuscates and classifies them → **FEC** reconstructs any lost
77	DATA frames from PARITY → the **resequencer** restores order across paths →
78	the in-order opaque datagram is delivered up to the engine as if from one
79	endpoint.
80	
81	Both ends run the same Bind; the diagram shows the dominant direction per role.
82	
83	## What we built — layer by layer
84	
85	Each bullet names the package (`internal/…`) that owns it.
86	
87	### Outer frame codec — `internal/frame`
88	
89	Wraps every outbound datagram in an outer bonding frame and defines the wire
90	format. Layout: a fresh 24-byte XChaCha20 nonce, then the **obfuscated** body
91	(`kind` byte ‖ per-kind header ‖ opaque payload), then an optional HMAC-SHA256
92	tag. Frame kinds:
93	
94	- **DATA** — carries an inner WireGuard datagram. Header has the outer sequence
95	  number, path id, and FEC group/index. **Unauthenticated** (no tag).
96	- **PARITY** — a Reed-Solomon parity shard for a FEC group. **Unauthenticated.**
97	- **PROBE** — telemetry/liveness. **PSK-HMAC authenticated**, carries a monotonic
98	  `ProbeSeq` + timestamp for anti-replay.
99	- **CONTROL** — reserved out-of-band control. **PSK-HMAC authenticated**, carries
100	  a MAC-covered monotonic `Seq`. *(See "Not yet built" — currently unwired.)*
101	
102	DPI resistance comes from here: the nonce randomizes every frame, the body is
103	XChaCha20-obfuscated, and there are **no magic bytes or fixed offsets** — the
104	wire is high-entropy UDP indistinguishable from noise (verified by
105	`internal/wireaudit` and `TestP5DPI`/`TestWireFormatAudit`). Overhead is small
106	and fixed: **DATA ≈ 17 bytes**, **PARITY ≈ 18 bytes** of header on top of the
107	nonce; the daemon subtracts this from the TUN MTU so there is no fragmentation.
108	
109	### The multipath Bind — `internal/bind`
110

# wanbond design

This document explains wanbond's architecture and, specifically, **exactly what
we built on top of the amneziawg-go WireGuard engine**. For setup and operation
see [install.md](install.md); for the front-door overview see the
[README](../README.md).

## Thesis

> Keep the WireGuard engine **unmodified**. Put **all** bonding logic in a custom
> `conn.Bind` beneath it, operating only on opaque, already-encrypted datagrams.

We embed [amneziawg-go](https://github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go) as a library
and use it exactly as intended — for TUN management, the Noise handshake, AEAD
encryption, key rotation (rekey), endpoint roaming, and keepalives. We add
**nothing** inside the engine. Everything wanbond does — multipath scheduling,
outer-frame obfuscation, forward error correction, receive resequencing, and
per-path telemetry — lives in an implementation of the engine's `conn.Bind`
transport interface, which the engine drives for every packet it sends and
receives.

This gives a clean separation: WireGuard owns confidentiality, integrity, and
authenticity of the *payload*; wanbond owns *delivery* across multiple lossy
paths, plus outer obfuscation. The Bind never inspects plaintext — it moves
opaque ciphertext datagrams.

## Why amneziawg-go (and not plain wireguard-go)

The architecture decision is closed: amneziawg-go over plain wireguard-go,
kcp-go, or quic-go. The reason is **DPI resistance** (requirement 6). AmneziaWG
adds configurable obfuscation to the WireGuard wire — junk packets (`jc`, `jmin`,
`jmax`), handshake junk prefixes (`s1`, `s2`), and custom magic headers
(`h1`–`h4`) — as *defense-in-depth* beneath our own outer obfuscation codec.
Using the engine as-is means WireGuard's battle-tested crypto and roaming come
for free while the obfuscation knobs are available when configured.

**Fork-lag hedge.** amneziawg-go is a fork of wireguard-go and can lag upstream
security/perf fixes. We contain that risk: the entire dependency on the engine's
`conn` package is isolated to **one file**, `internal/bind/bind.go`, via type
aliases (`Bind = conn.Bind`, `Endpoint = conn.Endpoint`,
`ReceiveFunc = conn.ReceiveFunc`). The `conn.Bind`/`conn.Endpoint` contracts are
byte-identical between the two forks, so swapping back to upstream wireguard-go
(dropping obfuscation) touches only that file.

## The data path

```
                          EDGE                                     CONCENTRATOR
   ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐      ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  applications / kernel routing         │      │       kernel routing / NAT onward      │
   │                 │                       │      │                 ▲                       │
   │           ┌─────▼──────┐  TUN           │      │           ┌─────┴──────┐  TUN           │
   │           │ WireGuard  │  (amneziawg-go)│      │           │ WireGuard  │                │
   │           │  engine    │  Noise/AEAD    │      │           │  engine    │                │
   │           └─────┬──────┘  rekey/roam    │      │           └─────▲──────┘                │
   │   opaque encrypted datagrams  │         │      │       opaque encrypted datagrams        │
   │           ┌─────▼──────────────────────┐│      │┌──────────────────────┴─────┐          │
   │           │   wanbond conn.Bind        ││      ││   wanbond conn.Bind        │          │
   │           │  ┌───────────────────────┐ ││      ││  ┌───────────────────────┐ │          │
   │  send ───►│  │ scheduler (sched)     │ ││      ││  │ resequencer (reseq)   │ │──► recv  │
   │           │  │ FEC encode (fec)      │ ││      ││  │ FEC decode (fec)      │ │          │
   │           │  │ frame codec (frame)   │ ││      ││  │ frame codec (frame)   │ │          │
   │           │  └──────────┬────────────┘ ││      ││  └──────────▲────────────┘ │          │
   │           │   per-path UDP sockets      ││      ││   per-path UDP sockets     │          │
   │           └──────┬───────────┬─────────┘│      │└──────▲───────────▲─────────┘          │
   └──────────────────┼───────────┼──────────┘      └───────┼───────────┼──────────────────┘
              starlink│    cellular│   ══════ real internet ══════│  path A    │ path B
```

**Send** (edge → concentrator): the engine hands the Bind an opaque encrypted
datagram → the **scheduler** picks the path(s) → **FEC** optionally emits parity
frames for the group → the **frame codec** wraps each datagram in an obfuscated
outer frame → it goes out the chosen per-path UDP socket.

**Receive** (concentrator side): frames arrive on the per-path sockets → the
**frame codec** de-obfuscates and classifies them → **FEC** reconstructs any lost
DATA frames from PARITY → the **resequencer** restores order across paths →
the in-order opaque datagram is delivered up to the engine as if from one
endpoint.

Both ends run the same Bind; the diagram shows the dominant direction per role.

## What we built — layer by layer

Each bullet names the package (`internal/…`) that owns it.

### Outer frame codec — `internal/frame`

Wraps every outbound datagram in an outer bonding frame and defines the wire
format. Layout: a fresh 24-byte XChaCha20 nonce, then the **obfuscated** body
(`kind` byte ‖ per-kind header ‖ opaque payload), then an optional HMAC-SHA256
tag. Frame kinds:

- **DATA** — carries an inner WireGuard datagram. Header has the outer sequence
  number, path id, and FEC group/index. **Unauthenticated** (no tag).
- **PARITY** — a Reed-Solomon parity shard for a FEC group. **Unauthenticated.**
- **PROBE** — telemetry/liveness. **PSK-HMAC authenticated**, carries a monotonic
  `ProbeSeq` + timestamp for anti-replay.
- **CONTROL** — reserved out-of-band control. **PSK-HMAC authenticated**, carries
  a MAC-covered monotonic `Seq`. *(See "Not yet built" — currently unwired.)*

DPI resistance comes from here: the nonce randomizes every frame, the body is
XChaCha20-obfuscated, and there are **no magic bytes or fixed offsets** — the
wire is high-entropy UDP indistinguishable from noise (verified by
`internal/wireaudit` and `TestP5DPI`/`TestWireFormatAudit`). Overhead is small
and fixed: **DATA ≈ 17 bytes**, **PARITY ≈ 18 bytes** of header on top of the
nonce; the daemon subtracts this from the TUN MTU so there is no fragmentation.

### The multipath Bind — `internal/bind`

content.content:
109	### The multipath Bind — `internal/bind`
110	
111	The heart of wanbond: the `conn.Bind` implementation the engine drives. It:
112	
113	- **presents one stable virtual endpoint per peer** to the engine while privately
114	  fanning out across the real per-path sockets beneath it (design rule **A1**, see
115	  [p0-findings.md §3](p0-findings.md)). The engine must never see per-packet
116	  endpoint churn, so every receive returns the *same* `*udpEndpoint`; the learned
117	  destination is held in an `atomic.Pointer` because the Bind writes it under its
118	  mutex while the engine reads it locklessly.
119	- **learns edge endpoints dynamically** — the concentrator needs no edge endpoint
120	  config; it discovers each path's (possibly NAT'd) source from inbound traffic,
121	  enabling real CGNAT traversal.
122	- **demuxes multiple peers by authenticated source binding (G4 multi-peer)** — on a
123	  concentrator with more than one configured peer, inbound datagrams are routed to
124	  the owning peer via `peerBySource`, an atomic-pointer source-address map
125	  populated only from authenticated PROBE frames. Each peer authenticates with its
126	  own per-peer `psk`: the first PROBE from a source that MAC-verifies under a
127	  peer's psk binds that source to that peer; subsequent DATA/PARITY frames from
128	  the same source are routed without re-authentication, keeping the receive hot
129	  path fast. With a single configured peer, a per-peer `psk` is **rejected** at
130	  config load (`config.validate`) and the top-level `psk` is the sole
131	  authenticator, byte-identical to pre-G4 behavior. Once a second peer is
132	  configured, per-peer `psk` becomes **required and pairwise-distinct**, and the
133	  top-level `psk` — still required by validation — **authenticates no peer**
134	  (`device.Up` feeds only each peer's own PSK, from `Config.PeerIdentities`, into
135	  the bind). Binding is learned only from PROBE frames — unauthenticated
136	  DATA/PARITY cannot establish or hijack a source-to-peer binding (D9/D11).
137	  Per-peer `name` is required in multi-peer mode and exposed as the metrics `peer`
138	  label for **additional** peers only; the first/primary peer's series always
139	  carry the empty label `peer=""` (D58).
140	- owns the **per-path UDP sockets**, byte counters, the send-path FEC `Admit`, the
141	  adaptive-FEC tick loop, and the wiring that hands frames to the scheduler and
142	  the resequencer.
143	- classifies each outbound batch by inner WireGuard message type (parameterized by
144	  the configured AmneziaWG obfuscation profile — custom `h1`–`h4` magic headers
145	  and `s1`/`s2` junk prefixes) so control frames can be treated specially by the
146	  pacer.
147	- **tolerates a not-yet-assignable `source_addr` at startup** (`Open()`). A path
148	  whose *well-formed* `source_addr` no interface holds yet — a mobile edge booting
149	  before its 5G modem has a DHCP lease, Starlink mid-obstruction — makes
150	  `net.ListenUDP` return `EADDRNOTAVAIL`. Rather than tear the whole bond down,
151	  `Open()` brings the tunnel up on the paths that **do** bind and *defers* the
152	  unbindable ones: a deferred path is recorded (with its boot prober) and left
153	  `Down` — its prober never echoes, so the scheduler excludes it, exactly as the
154	  runtime path-down model treats a live-but-silent path. Hard guards: if **zero**
155	  paths bind, `Open()` still fails fatally (no transport ⇒ no tunnel); a
156	  **malformed** `source_addr` remains a hard config-load error (`config.validate`
157	  rejects it at load); and any bind error that is **not** `EADDRNOTAVAIL`
158	  (`EADDRINUSE`, permission) stays fatal. Startup and the runtime model are
159	  symmetric: a SIGHUP reload that introduces a not-yet-assignable path *defers* it
160	  the same way (`AddPath`), a reload that keeps a deferred path is a no-op for it
161	  (`PathNames` reports the durable membership, deferred paths included), and a reload
162	  that drops one retires it (`RemovePath`) — so a deferred path never regresses the
163	  SIGHUP-no-op invariant.
164	- **reconciles a deferred path in the background** (`StartReconcileLoop`, T55). A
165	  device-lifecycle goroutine (started after the first `Open`, stopped before `Close`
166	  by the same `Tunnel.Close` that stops the probe loop — no goroutine leak) polls the
167	  deferred set at `DefaultReconcileInterval` (1 s) and re-attempts each deferred
168	  path's bind. When a path's `source_addr` **becomes assignable** (its interface/
169	  address appears — the 5G modem finally got its DHCP lease), the reconcile **binds
170	  and promotes** it to a live path: it enters `m.paths`, the scheduler (as a new
171	  lowest-priority path), and its own reader, **reusing the preserved boot prober** so
172	  the path keeps its reserved id-stamp (no renumber, no peer-reflector collision) —
173	  so the tunnel starts using it WITHOUT a `Close→Open` restart, and the scheduler
174	  promotes it to active by the SAME liveness path as any runtime `AddPath`. A path
175	  that still cannot bind stays deferred and is retried; a path REMOVED before it binds
176	  (`RemovePath`) is dropped from the deferred set and never promoted. Everything runs
177	  under `m.mu`, so it serializes with `Send`/`Close`/`AddPath`/`RemovePath` and is a
178	  no-op on a closed bind. **Mechanism:** a bounded periodic poll, chosen over
179	  event-driven netlink route/addr subscription (`vishvananda/netlink AddrSubscribe`)
180	  because netlink is not an existing dependency and the deferred set is normally empty
181	  — so the steady-state tick is a single mutex-guarded length check. The full
182	  absent-then-added path flow over a real interface is validated by a netns e2e (T60).
183	
184	This package is also the **amnezia boundary** (`bind.go`, above).
185	
186	### Send-side scheduler — `internal/sched`
187	
188	Decides which path(s) a frame goes out. Two policies:
189	
190	- **active-backup** (default) — one active path; instant failover to the backup on
191	  liveness loss; the metered link stays idle (data-thrift) until needed.
192	- **weighted aggregation** (opt-in) — striped/weighted send-weighted-round-robin
193	  across paths, with a Mathis-proxy path quality signal (`1/(RTT·√loss)`),
194	  three-region hysteresis to avoid flapping, and load-based engage/disengage.
195	
196	**Pacing** (per-path token buckets) is a scheduler feature that is **off by
197	default** and, when enabled, exempts WireGuard control frames from shedding so
198	overload cannot starve rekey. When pacing is enabled the per-path pace can be
199	sized from an **operator-declared** per-link bandwidth (`link_bandwidth` +
200	`link_rtt` on each `[[paths]]`): at config load `SizePacingFromBDP` derives the
201	scheduler's `per_path_capacity_fps` and `pacing_burst_frames` from the
202	bandwidth-delay product, sized to the **slowest declared link** (the shared pace
203	must not exceed the bottleneck). This is operator-*declared*, not runtime
204	auto-tuning — the value is fixed at load. With pacing off (the default) a declared
205	bandwidth is inert and the synthetic default pace is kept. See "Not yet built" for
206	why pacing stays off by default.
207	
208	**Sizing from the bandwidth-delay product.** The BDP algorithm (`SizePacingFromBDP`,
209	internal/config) sizes the pacing parameters as follows:
210	
211	- **`capacity_fps`** (frames/second): `bandwidth_bits_per_sec / (8 * avg_wire_frame_bytes)`.
212	  The rate at which the link sustains datagrams; frames arrive at this rate or the
213	  token bucket drains.
214	- **`burst_frames`** (frame count): `capacity_fps * rtt_seconds` ≡ `bandwidth * rtt / (8 * frame_size)`.
215	  The maximum burst (number of frames) the bucket can hold — one RTT's worth of
216	  in-flight data. Equivalently, the bandwidth-delay product (in bytes) divided by
217	  the average wire-frame size.
218	
219	The operator measures two values per link (see [install.md §3a](install.md#3a-tuning-per-link-bandwidth-and-pacing)):
220	**`link_bandwidth`** (bits/s, e.g. `"50Mbit"`) and **`link_rtt`** (latency in
221	milliseconds, e.g. `"21ms"`). The idle RTT is the baseline; pacing bounds the
222	queue so RTT under load stays near the idle value, preventing bufferbloat
223	(excessive delay inflation). If heterogeneous links are bonded (different
224	bandwidths), the operator declares all of them; the scheduler uses the bottleneck
225	(slowest link) to size the shared per-path pace, because any link can be the
226	path for a given packet.
227	
228	**Conservative sizing.** The wire-frame size used in the denominator is the full
229	path MTU (1500 bytes), the conservative floor for frame size. This produces a
230	frame rate that never over-paces a path; smaller average frames (headers,
231	fragmentation) would permit higher rates, but taking the worst case (full MTU)
232	ensures the pacer does not let the link overfill. Measurement on real links is
233	essential to validate that the declared bandwidth and RTT reflect the actual
234	link properties; the netns fixture is CPU-bound and cannot build the standing
235	queues pacing is designed to control (see [manual-checklist.md §P0](manual-checklist.md#p0--spike--baseline)).
236
